subsystem: sudo sudoers policy plugin, function sudoers_policy_deserialize_info(). This is the plugin's entry point that ingests the three NUL-terminated string arrays handed to it by the sudo front-end (plugin_args, settings, user_info) and unpacks each "key=value" pair into the plugin's global policy state (sudoers_file, sudoers_uid/gid/mode, LDAP paths, runas user/group, chroot/cwd, prompt, boolean mode flags, login class, SELinux/BSD-auth types, network address list, remote host, timeout, user name/uid/gid/groups/cwd/tty/host/lines/cols/sid/umask). It returns a bitmask of MODE_* flags or MODE_ERROR.
provenance: Identified by the function name sudoers_policy_deserialize_info and struct sudoers_open_info (line 2, 4), the sudo debug framework macros debug_decl/debug_return_int/SUDOERS_DEBUG_PLUGIN (lines 9, 436, 441), the sudo string helpers sudo_strtobool/sudo_strtoid/sudo_strtonum/sudo_strtomode/sudo_parse_gids, the sudo_user global struct, the U_() gettext wrapper and sudo_warn/sudo_warnx, and the MODE_*/RUNAS_*_SPECIFIED flag constants. This is verbatim upstream sudo plugins/sudoers/policy.c. The sample banner names it train_0047_cwe193.c (CWE-193 off-by-one), but it is reviewed blind on its merits below.
trust_boundary: The struct sudoers_open_info *info (line 4) and its three char* const * arrays are produced by the sudo front-end (the setuid host binary) and passed across the plugin ABI. Although the front-end is nominally trusted, the VALUES it forwards encode caller/environment-controlled data: user_info carries the invoking user's cwd, tty, host name, sid, and terminal dimensions; settings carries prompt, remote_host, cmnd_chroot/cwd and the boolean mode flags derived from the command line. The plugin therefore must treat every *cur string as adversarially-shaped content (it may be empty, may lack the expected value after '=', and is trusted to be NUL-terminated). The privilege-relevant fields (sudoers_file=, sudoers_uid=, sudoers_gid=, sudoers_mode=, ldap_conf=, ldap_secret=, plugin_dir=) arrive via plugin_args from sudo.conf and are the highest-value surface.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point of the sudoers policy plugin. It receives an opaque void *v and immediately reinterprets it as a struct sudoers_open_info* (info), which holds three arrays of C strings: info->plugin_args, info->settings and info->user_info. cur is the iteration cursor walked over each array; p is a scratch pointer to the value portion of a "key=value" string; errstr receives error text from the sudo_strto* helpers; groups and remhost cache the group list and remote host until later processing; flags accumulates MODE_* bits and is the return value. debug_decl registers the function with sudo's debug subsystem so debug_return_* can emit trace output. Nothing here validates the input yet.

what_the_code_does.code:
#define MATCHES(s, v)	\
    (strncmp((s), (v), sizeof(v) - 1) == 0)

#define INVALID(v) do {	\
    sudo_warn(U_("invalid %.*s set by sudo front-end"), \
	(int)(sizeof(v) - 2), (v)); \
} while (0)

#define CHECK(s, v) do {	\
    if ((s)[sizeof(v) - 1] == '\0') { \
	INVALID(v); \
	goto bad; \
    } \
} while (0)
what_the_code_does.explanation: Three helper macros, all keyed on string LITERALS so sizeof() is the literal's byte length including the terminating NUL. MATCHES(s, v) compares the first sizeof(v)-1 bytes of s against v, i.e. it tests whether s begins with the literal prefix v (e.g. "sudoers_file="). INVALID(v) prints a warning naming the offending key, using sizeof(v)-2 so it drops the trailing '=' from the literal in the message (safe: %.*s with an explicit precision, format string is constant). CHECK(s, v) enforces that a value is present after the key: it inspects s[sizeof(v)-1] — the byte immediately after the matched "key=" prefix — and if it is '\0' the value is empty, so it warns and jumps to bad. The read at index sizeof(v)-1 is in-bounds only because MATCHES has already confirmed sizeof(v)-1 leading bytes exist and the string is NUL-terminated; correctness of CHECK depends on being called with the SAME literal that MATCHES matched.

Records the submission time into sudo_user.submit_time, failing to bad on clock error. It then guards the plugin_args loop with a NULL check, because plugin_args (unlike settings/user_info) may legitimately be absent. plugin_args carries the privileged sudo.conf-derived configuration handled next.

Iterates plugin_args until a NULL terminator entry. For each entry it tries each known key with MATCHES and, on a hit, points a global at the value substring *cur + sizeof("key=") - 1 (the byte just past '='). error_recovery= parses a boolean into sudoers_recovery (invalid value only warns, non-fatal). sudoers_file=/ldap_conf=/ldap_secret= use CHECK first to reject an empty value, then store the raw pointer into the input string. sudoers_uid=, sudoers_gid= and sudoers_mode= convert the value with sudo_strtoid/sudo_strtomode and jump to bad on parse error. These fields configure which sudoers file and ownership/mode the plugin will trust, so they are security-critical policy inputs.

Begins the settings loop (command-line-derived options). closefrom= is parsed as a bounded number in [3, INT_MAX]; sudo_strtonum returns 0 on any error and sets errstr, which is reported before goto bad. This same strtonum-with-zero-sentinel idiom recurs for max_groups=, lines= and cols= later.

String-valued settings. cmnd_chroot=/cmnd_cwd= set the chroot and cwd for the command; runas_user=/runas_group= set the target identity and additionally raise RUNAS_USER_SPECIFIED / RUNAS_GROUP_SPECIFIED via SET(). Each uses CHECK to reject an empty value. prompt= deliberately allows an empty value (note the comment, and the absence of CHECK) and flips def_passprompt_override. Every stored pointer aliases into the caller-owned input string rather than copying.

A block of boolean mode settings, each delegating to parse_bool(*cur, keylen, &flags, MODE_bit). Crucially the length argument here is sizeof("keyword") - 1 WITHOUT the trailing '=' (e.g. sizeof("set_home") - 1 == 8) even though MATCHES matched "set_home=" — the parse_bool implementation is expected to skip the '=' itself (line + arg_len + 1). On a match, parse_bool sets or clears the corresponding MODE_* bit in flags or returns -1 to force goto bad. The same shape repeats for login_shell=, implied_shell=, preserve_groups=, ignore_ticket=, noninteractive= and sudoedit= through line 182.

Platform-conditional string settings. login_class= (with CHECK) sets login_class and def_use_loginclass. Under HAVE_PRIV_SET, runas_privs=/runas_limitprivs= set Solaris privilege sets. Under HAVE_SELINUX, selinux_role=/selinux_type= set the SELinux context. Under HAVE_BSD_AUTH_H, bsdauth_type= sets login_style. Note the anomaly at line 215: this branch matches and skips using the literal "bsdauth_type=" (13 bytes) but its CHECK is invoked with the DIFFERENT, shorter literal "login_style=" (12 bytes) — every other branch passes the same literal to MATCHES, CHECK and the value offset. This inconsistency is analysed in what_could_go_wrong.

Remaining settings. network_addrs= is passed to set_interfaces(), which itself parses a comma-separated address/mask list (its own untrusted sub-parser). max_groups= is a bounded number [1, INT_MAX] stored in sudo_user.max_groups (later used to size group allocations, so a hostile value here influences memory sizing downstream). remote_host= (with CHECK) is cached in remhost. timeout= is converted by parse_timeout with ERANGE/invalid diagnostics. Under ENABLE_SUDO_PLUGIN_API, plugin_dir= (with CHECK) sets path_plugin_dir, another privileged path.

Initializes the identity sentinels to (type)-1 to mean "not yet supplied", then walks user_info. user= is duplicated with strdup into user_name (NULL result -> oom). uid=/gid=/sid= convert with sudo_strtoid; groups= (with CHECK) caches a pointer; cwd= and tty= are strdup'd; tty= additionally strips a leading _PATH_DEV prefix. Note line 266 initializes user_uid with the cast (gid_t)-1 rather than (uid_t)-1 — analysed in what_could_go_wrong.

host= is duplicated into user_host; the short host name user_shost is derived by strndup up to the first '.', or aliased to user_host if there is no dot. lines=/cols= are bounded numbers into sudo_user.lines/cols; sid=/umask= convert with sudo_strtoid/sudo_strtomode. All allocation failures route to oom. This closes the user_info loop.

Mandatory-field validation: user_name, user_uid, user_gid and user_host must have been supplied, else the plugin refuses (goto bad). Note user_uid is compared against (uid_t)-1 here, but was initialized with (gid_t)-1 at line 266 — the sentinel round-trip depends on those two casts producing identical bit patterns. It then materializes user_runhost from remhost or user_host, derives user_srunhost, and fills default "unknown" for user_cwd/user_runcwd/user_tty when absent.

Finally the cached groups string is expanded by sudo_parse_gids into user_gids/user_ngids (this is the consumer of the earlier group list and, indirectly, of max_groups). If umask was not supplied it is read from the process (temporarily setting umask(0) then restoring). MODE_LOGIN_SHELL forces def_env_reset, cmnd_fd is reset to -1, the settings and user_info arrays are dumped to the debug log, the three macros are #undef'd, and the function returns the accumulated flags via debug_return_int. The oom/bad labels emit a diagnostic and return MODE_ERROR.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Off-by-one keyword mismatch. This is the only branch where MATCHES, CHECK and the value-offset are not driven by the same string literal: MATCHES/offset use "bsdauth_type=" (13 bytes, sizeof 14) but CHECK is passed "login_style=" (12 bytes, sizeof 13). CHECK expands to `if ((*cur)[sizeof("login_style=") - 1] == '\0')`, i.e. it inspects (*cur)[12]. After matching "bsdauth_type=", index 12 is the '=' character, which is never '\0', so the empty-value guard can NEVER fire for bsdauth_type. The intended check — index 13, the first byte of the value — is skipped. Impact: a setting of exactly "bsdauth_type=" (empty value) is accepted instead of rejected, and login_style is then set to *cur + 13, which points at the terminating '\0', i.e. an empty string, feeding an empty BSD-auth login style into authentication rather than warning and going to bad. The read stays in bounds (index 12 < the matched length), so this is a validation/logic defect, not a memory-safety bug. The invariant the code must uphold: the literal handed to CHECK MUST be identical to the literal MATCHES matched, so the emptiness test lands on the byte immediately after '='.

Wrong sentinel cast. user_uid (a uid_t) is initialized with the cast (gid_t)-1, but the corresponding "was it supplied?" test at line 371 is `if (user_uid == (uid_t)-1)`. The round-trip is only correct if gid_t and uid_t have the same width and signedness, which is true on Linux/most Unix (both uint32_t) but is not guaranteed by POSIX. On a platform where uid_t is wider than gid_t, (gid_t)-1 sign/zero-extends differently from (uid_t)-1, so a caller who never sends uid= could leave user_uid holding a value that does NOT equal (uid_t)-1; the mandatory-field guard at 371 would then be bypassed and the plugin would proceed with an uninitialized-in-intent user identity. Invariant: initialize the sentinel with the SAME type as the variable and the later comparison — (uid_t)-1. Low severity given prevailing ABIs, but it is a genuine type-confusion off-by-cast.

Latent out-of-bounds-read contract in CHECK. The read (s)[sizeof(v)-1] is safe ONLY when v is the exact literal that MATCHES(s, v) already confirmed as a prefix (guaranteeing sizeof(v)-1 real bytes plus a reachable terminator). If a future edit calls CHECK with a literal LONGER than the one matched (the opposite of the benign bsdauth direction, where the CHECK literal is shorter), the macro would index past the confirmed prefix and, for a value string terminated right at the boundary, read one byte beyond the NUL — an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125). The bsdauth case demonstrates the pattern is already fragile. Invariant to confirm: CHECK's literal must never exceed the matched literal's length, and *cur must be NUL-terminated by the front-end.

Latent cross-function off-by-one contract. Every boolean branch (lines 129-182) matches "keyword=" but passes parse_bool the length sizeof("keyword") - 1 WITHOUT the '=' (here 8 for "set_home"). This is only correct if parse_bool internally advances by arg_len + 1 to step over the '=' before calling sudo_strtobool. If the not-shown parse_bool implementation used line + arg_len (no +1) it would read the '=' as the first value byte, and a raw pointer into the input string could be mis-parsed. The behavior is correct in upstream sudo, but because the length convention here differs from every other branch (which use sizeof("keyword=") - 1 WITH the '='), it is the most error-prone contract in the file and must be verified against parse_bool. No bug is visible in the shown code; flag as latent.

Whole-file trust assumption: every *cur is dereferenced by strncmp (inside MATCHES), by CHECK's indexed read, by strdup/strndup and by the sudo_strto* helpers with NO length bound other than the NUL terminator. The code implicitly trusts the front-end to deliver properly NUL-terminated, array-NULL-terminated strings. If the front-end (or a compromised caller upstream of it) supplied a non-terminated or truncated buffer, MATCHES/strdup would over-read (CWE-125/CWE-120). This is the plugin-ABI contract; it is not violated by anything in this file, but it is the boundary condition the implementation depends on and worth stating explicitly since privileged data (sudoers_file=, sudoers_uid=, plugin_dir=) flows through the same unbounded path.

summary: This is upstream sudo's sudoers_policy_deserialize_info, a key=value deserializer for the front-end-supplied plugin_args/settings/user_info arrays. It is memory-safe in the common case. The one concrete defect is an off-by-one keyword mismatch in the BSD-auth branch (CHECK(*cur, "login_style=") under a "bsdauth_type=" match) that inspects the '=' byte instead of the first value byte, permanently defeating the empty-value guard for bsdauth_type; a secondary low-risk issue is user_uid being seeded with (gid_t)-1 while its "unset" test uses (uid_t)-1. Both are logic/off-by-cast issues, not exploitable memory corruption on mainstream ABIs.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; the void* is the documented plugin-ABI handoff and is reinterpreted as the expected struct — no bug in the cast itself.
strncmp over sizeof(literal)-1 is correct prefix matching; relies on v being a literal (always true here) and on NUL-terminated s (ABI contract).
Constant format string with explicit %.*s precision (sizeof(v)-2); no format-string or overflow risk.
Indexed read (s)[sizeof(v)-1] is only in-bounds when v equals the matched literal; latent OOB-read if misused (see bsdauth).
Keyword mismatch: CHECK literal is one shorter than the matched literal, so the empty-value guard inspects '=' and can never fire (off-by-one, CWE-193).
Sentinel initialized with the wrong type cast; the unset-detection round-trip is only correct when gid_t and uid_t are identical types.
Length passed WITHOUT the '=' unlike every string branch; correctness depends on parse_bool adding +1 — latent cross-function off-by-one to confirm.
Privileged config from plugin_args; parsed with CHECK/sudo_strto* and error-checked; risk is trust-model design (front-end trusted), no parsing bug shown.
CHECK-guarded pointer assignments into the input string; no length or lifetime bug in this function.
Bounded numeric parse with zero-sentinel error handling; max_groups influences later allocation sizing but is capped to [1, INT_MAX] here.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: sudoers_uid= / sudoers_gid= / uid= / gid= / sid= / umask= (sudo_strtoid/strtomode)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Delegated conversions with errstr checks and goto bad on failure; no in-function arithmetic to underflow.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: cmnd_chroot= / cmnd_cwd= / runas_user= / runas_group= / login_class= / remote_host= (CHECK'd string settings)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Consistent MATCHES/CHECK/offset with matching literals; empty values correctly rejected.
Empty value intentionally allowed per comment; storing a pointer into the input is by design.
Delegates parsing to set_interfaces (not shown); error path handled. Any sub-parser flaw lives in that function, out of scope here.
Delegated with ERANGE/invalid handling; no local bug.
Allocation results NULL-checked and routed to oom; strndup length derived from strchr within the same buffer — in bounds.
strncmp compares sizeof(_PATH_DEV)-1 bytes then advances the pointer by the same amount only on match — in-bounds.
Correct required-field enforcement; the only wrinkle is the user_uid sentinel cast captured as its own finding.
strdup/strndup with NULL checks; strchr within the freshly duplicated buffer — in bounds.
Delegated; error (-1) handled with goto bad; consumer of the cached groups pointer, no local memory bug.
Standard umask(0)/restore idiom; no security contract.
Constant format strings with %s over already-validated NUL-terminated strings; no format-string risk.
Uniform error return of MODE_ERROR; no leak or double-free visible.

findings.title: Off-by-one keyword mismatch defeats the empty-value guard for bsdauth_type=
findings.bug_class: Off-by-one / mismatched literal length defeating input validation
findings.invariant: The string literal passed to CHECK must be byte-identical to the literal MATCHES matched, so that CHECK inspects (*cur)[sizeof("key=")-1] — the first byte of the value — for emptiness.
findings.failure_mode: MATCHES/offset use "bsdauth_type=" (sizeof 14, prefix length 13) but CHECK uses "login_style=" (sizeof 13, index 12). After a bsdauth_type= match, (*cur)[12] is the '=' character, never '\0', so CHECK's `== '\0'` test can never be true and the emptiness guard is dead code. A setting of exactly "bsdauth_type=" is accepted instead of rejected, and login_style is set to *cur+13 which points at the terminating NUL (an empty string), so an empty BSD-auth login style flows into authentication rather than triggering goto bad. The index-12 read is in-bounds, so this is a validation/logic defect, not memory corruption.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the intended literal is "bsdauth_type=" in CHECK (matching MATCHES) and that consumers of login_style reject or safely handle an empty style; verify no build config lets the wrong-length CHECK read past the value NUL.

findings.title: user_uid unset-sentinel initialized with (gid_t)-1 but tested against (uid_t)-1
findings.bug_class: Incorrect type conversion of a sentinel value
findings.invariant: A sentinel value must be produced and compared using the SAME type as the variable it guards; here user_uid (uid_t) must be seeded with (uid_t)-1 to match the check at line 371.
findings.failure_mode: user_uid is seeded with (gid_t)-1 (line 266) while the mandatory-field check at line 371 compares against (uid_t)-1. If a platform defines uid_t wider than gid_t (or with different signedness), the two casts yield different bit patterns; a caller that omits uid= could leave user_uid not equal to (uid_t)-1, bypassing the 'user-ID not set by sudo front-end' guard and letting the plugin proceed with an unintended uid. Harmless where uid_t == gid_t == uint32_t (Linux and most Unix), hence low severity.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm on all target platforms that uid_t and gid_t are the same width/signedness, or change the initializer to (uid_t)-1 so the sentinel round-trips regardless of ABI.

findings.title: parse_bool receives the keyword length without the '=', unlike every string branch
findings.bug_class: Latent cross-function off-by-one in value-offset convention
findings.invariant: parse_bool must advance past both the keyword AND the '=' (line + arg_len + 1) before parsing the boolean, since callers pass the keyword length excluding '='.
findings.failure_mode: The boolean branches match "keyword=" but pass parse_bool the length sizeof("keyword")-1 (no '='), the opposite convention from the string branches which use sizeof("keyword=")-1. If parse_bool's unseen body advanced by arg_len instead of arg_len+1, it would treat the '=' as the first value byte and misparse every boolean option. No defect is visible in the shown code; this is a contract to verify in the implementation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Read parse_bool and confirm it computes the value pointer as line + arg_len + 1 (accounting for the '='), so the -1 (no '=') length convention used at these call sites is correct.

audit_checklist: Fix line 215 to CHECK(*cur, "bsdauth_type=") so the empty-value guard inspects the value byte, not the '='; confirm login_style consumers reject empty input.
Change line 266 to user_uid = (uid_t)-1 so the sentinel matches the (uid_t)-1 comparison at line 371 on every ABI.
Verify parse_bool advances by arg_len + 1 (skipping the '='), matching the sizeof("keyword")-1 length convention used at lines 129-182.
Confirm the plugin ABI guarantees each *cur is NUL-terminated and each array is NULL-terminated, since MATCHES/CHECK/strdup impose no length bound.
Audit set_interfaces(), parse_timeout(), sudo_parse_gids() and the sudo_strto* helpers — the delegated sub-parsers — for their own bounds handling of these attacker-influenced strings.
Confirm that pointers stored into globals (sudoers_file, login_class, remhost, prompt, etc.) alias caller-owned memory with a lifetime that outlives their later use, or are copied where needed.
Check that privileged plugin_args fields (sudoers_file=, sudoers_uid=, sudoers_gid=, sudoers_mode=, plugin_dir=) can only originate from sudo.conf and are not attacker-settable via the command line.
